questions.question: Which developer forms require the part surface to be DRY before they are applied?
options: Water-soluble and water-suspendible
Special forms only
Dry powder and nonaqueous wet
All developer forms
questions.explanation: Aqueous developers go onto wet parts ahead of the dryer; dry powder and nonaqueous wet developers need a dry surface, so on a line they follow the drying oven.

questions.question: Why do penetrant dip tanks on a processing line carry covers?
options: To limit evaporation and keep contamination out of the bath
To keep the penetrant warm overnight
To block UV light from pre-exposing the dye
Covers are decorative and optional in every shop
questions.explanation: An open tank collects dust, water, and stray chemistry while losing volatiles to evaporation; lids are a basic contamination and material-condition control.

questions.question: Which set of inspection-booth conditions is acceptable for Type I evaluation under typical procedures?
options: UV-A of 600 µW/cm² at the part, ambient 1 fc, no dark adaptation
UV-A of 1,200 µW/cm² at the part, ambient 1.5 fc, several minutes of dark adaptation
UV-A of 1,200 µW/cm² at the part, ambient 10 fc, dark adaptation taken
UV-A of 3,000 µW/cm² at the lamp face, ambient light not measured
questions.explanation: All conditions must pass together: at least about 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface, ambient white light at about 2 fc or less, and adapted eyes. The other options each fail one.

questions.question: What lighting does Type II visible-dye evaluation typically require?
options: Ambient white light of 2 fc or less at the surface
UV-A of at least 1,000 µW/cm² of white light
About 20 lux at the examination surface
At least 100 fc (about 1,000 lux) of white light at the examination surface
questions.explanation: Visible red-on-white contrast needs abundant white light — typically at least 100 fc (about 1,000 lux) measured at the surface with a photometer.

questions.question: A power blip shuts down the booth's mercury-vapor UV lamp mid-shift. What must happen before inspection resumes?
options: The lamp must cool, restrike, and warm to stabilized output — commonly several minutes — and intensity should be re-verified
Nothing; mercury lamps restart instantly at full output
Inspection continues immediately under white light
The booth curtain is opened to add working light
questions.explanation: A hot mercury arc must cool before it restrikes, then warm up and stabilize again; verifying intensity afterward is the only proof the booth is back within limits.

questions.question: Why are photochromic (self-darkening) lenses prohibited during fluorescent evaluation?
options: They block all UV from reaching the part
They scratch too easily in shop service
Booth UV darkens them, cutting the inspector's visual sensitivity exactly when it is needed
They fluoresce and create false indications
questions.explanation: Ultraviolet is what triggers photochromic darkening, so booth UV tints the lenses and robs the dark-adapted eye of the dim indication light it needs.

questions.question: Beyond the routine start-of-shift check, when must UV-A intensity be re-verified?
options: Only at the annual quality audit
After a bulb or lamp change, after a lamp is dropped or relocated, and whenever output is in doubt
Only after a part is rejected
Never, as long as the radiometer is in calibration
questions.explanation: Typical procedures tie re-verification to events that can change output — bulb changes, mechanical shock, relocation — plus any doubt; a calibrated meter does not make the lamp immune.

questions.question: What may be used for penetrant testing of hardware destined for liquid-oxygen service?
options: Only penetrant materials qualified as LOX-compatible — ordinary hydrocarbon residues can react violently with oxygen
Any low-viscosity penetrant that rinses cleanly
Any Type II penetrant, since the dye is inert
Standard water-washable penetrant with a double post-clean
questions.explanation: Oxygen systems demand LOX-compatible qualified materials; conventional oil-based residues in contact with liquid or gaseous oxygen are an explosion hazard no post-clean can certify away.

questions.question: During the daily check you spot glowing specks in the dry developer bin under UV. What do they mean, and what do you do?
options: Normal powder fluorescence; continue processing
Moisture contamination; oven-dry the powder and reuse it
The UV lamp is set too high; reduce the intensity
Penetrant contamination that will cause false indications; replace the developer
questions.explanation: Fluorescent specks are penetrant tracked into the powder; every speck can transfer to a part as a false indication, so contaminated dry developer is discarded, not stirred or dried.

questions.question: Which statement about lipophilic (Method B) emulsifier is correct?
options: It is mixed with water at 5–35 percent and preceded by a water pre-rinse
It is used as supplied; the part is dipped and drained without agitation, and emulsification contact time is the critical control
It is sprayed at high pressure to scrub penetrant from the surface
It is applied after the developer to brighten indications
questions.explanation: Lipophilic emulsifier works by timed diffusion into the penetrant — dip, drain, no agitation, rinse exactly when the established contact time expires. The 5–35 percent water mix describes hydrophilic Method D.
